note: Held-out gold set for the AI reasoning grader. Each item is a student's free-response argument on an MCAT question, with a human-assigned label. NONE of these appear in any prompt the app sends, so this is a true held-out test. Labels: sound (reasoning is correct and complete), partially_sound (right idea, gaps or minor errors), flawed (wrong reasoning / wrong conclusion). The grader is scored on exact-label accuracy and on a stricter 'safe' collapse (flawed vs not-flawed) since the costly error is calling flawed reasoning sound.

cutoff.explanation: Ship the grader only if exact-label accuracy >= 70% AND it misses no more than 20% of genuinely flawed reasoning (never praise a wrong argument).

items.question: A wave doubles in frequency while its speed is held constant. Its wavelength:
items.explanation: v = fλ. With v held constant, doubling f must halve λ.
items.student_reasoning: Speed equals frequency times wavelength, and speed is constant, so if frequency doubles the wavelength has to halve to keep the product the same.

items.question: A wave doubles in frequency while its speed is held constant. Its wavelength:
items.explanation: v = fλ. With v held constant, doubling f must halve λ.
items.student_reasoning: Frequency and wavelength both describe the wave getting bigger, so if frequency goes up wavelength goes up too.

items.question: Which amino acid is most likely buried in the hydrophobic core of a globular protein?
items.explanation: Valine has a nonpolar side chain, so it is sequestered away from water in the protein core.
items.student_reasoning: Valine is nonpolar so it hates water and gets buried inside away from the aqueous environment.

items.question: Which amino acid is most likely buried in the hydrophobic core of a globular protein?
items.explanation: Valine has a nonpolar side chain, so it is sequestered away from water in the protein core.
items.student_reasoning: I picked valine because it is a small amino acid and small things fit in the middle.

items.question: During intense exercise, muscle regenerates NAD+ anaerobically primarily via:
items.explanation: Fermentation reduces pyruvate to lactate, oxidizing NADH back to NAD+ so glycolysis can continue without oxygen.
items.student_reasoning: Without oxygen the cell converts pyruvate to lactate, which turns NADH back into NAD+ so glycolysis keeps running.

items.question: During intense exercise, muscle regenerates NAD+ anaerobically primarily via:
items.explanation: Fermentation reduces pyruvate to lactate, oxidizing NADH back to NAD+ so glycolysis can continue without oxygen.
items.student_reasoning: Oxidative phosphorylation makes the most ATP so it must be what regenerates NAD+ during hard exercise.

items.question: A 2 kg cart accelerates at 3 m/s^2. The net force on it is:
items.student_reasoning: Force is mass times acceleration, so 2 times 3 is 6 newtons.

items.question: A 2 kg cart accelerates at 3 m/s^2. The net force on it is:
items.student_reasoning: I added the mass and acceleration, 2 plus 3 gives 5 newtons.

items.question: Which memory store has essentially unlimited capacity and the longest duration?
items.explanation: Long-term memory has no known capacity limit and can persist for years; the others are brief and limited.
items.student_reasoning: Long-term memory can hold information for years and has no real capacity limit, unlike the brief stores.

items.question: Which memory store has essentially unlimited capacity and the longest duration?
items.explanation: Long-term memory has no known capacity limit and can persist for years; the others are brief and limited.
items.student_reasoning: Working memory is where we keep everything we know so it must have the biggest capacity.

items.question: The resting membrane potential of a neuron is maintained primarily by:
items.explanation: The Na+/K+ ATPase pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in, sustaining the gradients behind the ~ -70 mV resting potential.
items.student_reasoning: The sodium potassium pump keeps the ion gradients that set the resting potential by pumping sodium out and potassium in.

items.question: The resting membrane potential of a neuron is maintained primarily by:
items.explanation: The Na+/K+ ATPase pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in, sustaining the gradients behind the ~ -70 mV resting potential.
items.student_reasoning: I know the pump is involved in neurons somehow so I picked it, but I am not sure exactly what it does.

items.question: In a solution at equilibrium, adding more product will:
items.explanation: By Le Chatelier's principle, adding product stresses the system, which shifts to consume product, i.e. toward reactants.
items.student_reasoning: Le Chatelier says the system relieves the stress, so adding product pushes it back toward reactants to use up the extra product.

items.question: In a solution at equilibrium, adding more product will:
items.explanation: By Le Chatelier's principle, adding product stresses the system, which shifts to consume product, i.e. toward reactants.
items.student_reasoning: More product means the forward reaction speeds up to make even more product.

items.question: A researcher finds a correlation between ice cream sales and drownings. The best interpretation is:
items.explanation: Correlation is not causation; a third variable (hot weather) plausibly increases both ice cream sales and swimming, hence drownings.
items.student_reasoning: Correlation doesn't mean causation. Hot weather likely increases both ice cream buying and swimming, so it's a confounding variable.

items.question: A researcher finds a correlation between ice cream sales and drownings. The best interpretation is:
items.explanation: Correlation is not causation; a third variable (hot weather) plausibly increases both ice cream sales and swimming, hence drownings.
items.student_reasoning: Since the two go up together, eating ice cream must be what causes people to drown.

items.question: Enzyme catalysis lowers which quantity for a reaction?
items.explanation: Enzymes lower activation energy, speeding the reaction; they do not change ΔG or the equilibrium constant.
items.student_reasoning: Enzymes give a lower-energy pathway, so they reduce the activation energy without changing the overall thermodynamics.

items.question: Enzyme catalysis lowers which quantity for a reaction?
items.explanation: Enzymes lower activation energy, speeding the reaction; they do not change ΔG or the equilibrium constant.
items.student_reasoning: Enzymes make reactions more favorable so they must lower the free energy change to make it happen.

items.question: Which best explains why oil and water do not mix?

choices.text: The entropy cost of ordering water around nonpolar oil

items.explanation: Nonpolar oil forces surrounding water into ordered cages, lowering entropy; separating minimizes this cost (the hydrophobic effect).
items.student_reasoning: Water has to form ordered structures around nonpolar oil which lowers entropy, so they separate to reduce that entropy penalty.

items.question: Which best explains why oil and water do not mix?

choices.text: The entropy cost of ordering water around nonpolar oil

items.explanation: Nonpolar oil forces surrounding water into ordered cages, lowering entropy; separating minimizes this cost (the hydrophobic effect).
items.student_reasoning: Oil is heavier than water so it just sinks and separates out.
